overall_topic: Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers :     Occupational Outlook Handbook: :     U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics
questions: Within the broad category of U.S. occupations that includes professionals who design applications, analyze systems, secure networks, administer databases, and test software for defects, identify the overall occupational group to which these roles belong. Using the most recent federal labor statistics, explain the group’s projected growth rate for 2024–2034, the average number of annual openings, and its median annual wage in 2024, including how that wage compares to the median for all occupations. Then, select three distinct occupations within this group and provide for each its typical entry-level education and 2024 median annual pay. Finally, identify the undergraduate field of study most directly associated with preparing workers for many of these roles and summarize what kind of labor market and occupational information is typically presented for that field.

Identify the U.S. occupational group that includes professionals who design computer applications as well as those who identify and report defects in software programs, and that is projected to grow 15% between 2024 and 2034. For this occupational group, provide: the 2024 median annual pay for application designers, the 2024 median annual pay for those who test and analyze software quality, the typical entry-level education required, whether related work experience or on-the-job training is typically required, the total number of jobs in 2024, the projected employment change from 2024 to 2034, the average number of annual openings over the decade, and a brief description of the common work environments in which these professionals are employed.

Within the U.S. labor statistics system, there is a single occupational grouping that combines professionals who design and build computer applications with those who test software and identify defects before release. Using publicly available federal occupational data for this combined category, summarize the following: the 2024 median annual pay for developers and for quality assurance analysts/testers separately; the overall 2024 median pay figure highlighted for the combined occupation; the typical entry-level education required; whether related work experience or on-the-job training is generally required; the total number of jobs reported for 2024; the projected employment growth rate from 2024 to 2034; the numeric employment change expected over that decade; and the average number of annual openings projected during the period. Present all requested figures and descriptions clearly.

rounds.answer_a:
The roles described belong to the **Computer and Information Technology Occupations** major occupational group in the U.S. labor force.[1] The Bureau of Labor Statistics projects this group to grow **11.7% from 2024 to 2034**, much faster than the average for all occupations, with about **356,700 openings per year** on average over the decade.[1] In **2024**, the group’s **median annual wage was $106,980**, compared with **$49,500 for all occupations**, meaning pay in this group was more than twice the overall median.[1]

Examples within this group include: **Software developers**, whose typical entry-level education is a **bachelor’s degree** and whose **2024 median annual pay was $133,080**; **Information security analysts**, also typically needing a **bachelor’s degree**, with **2024 median pay of $126,260**; and **Database administrators and architects**, again typically requiring a **bachelor’s degree**, with **2024 median pay of $127,230**.[2][3][4]

The undergraduate field most directly tied to preparing workers for many of these jobs is **Computer Science**.[5] BLS field-of-degree information typically presents labor-market and occupational data such as the **most common occupations held by graduates**, **unemployment rates**, **median earnings**, and sometimes comparisons by **degree level**, helping show how a field connects to jobs and pay outcomes.[5] Together, these statistics show a large, fast-growing, and comparatively high-paying occupational group supported strongly by computer science training.[1][5]

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Computer and Information Technology Occupations.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/home.htm

[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm

[3] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Information Security Analysts.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/information-security-analysts.htm

[4] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Database Administrators and Architects.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/database-administrators.htm

[5] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Field of Degree page, “Computer Science.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/field-of-degree/computer-science/computer-science-field-of-degree.htm
rounds.answer_b:
The broad occupational group is **“Computer and Information Technology Occupations”** in the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) system.[1] For 2024–2034, this group is projected to grow **22%**, “much faster than average,” with about **421,400 openings each year** on average.[1] In 2024, the **median annual wage** for computer and information technology occupations was **$119,840**, compared with **$48,060** for all occupations, meaning the group’s median pay is roughly 2.5 times higher than the overall median.[1]

Three distinct occupations in this group include:

1. **Software Developers and Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers**  
   - Typical entry-level education: **Bachelor’s degree**  
   - 2024 median pay: **$138,130**[2]  

2. **Information Security Analysts**  
   - Typical entry-level education: **Bachelor’s degree**  
   - 2024 median pay: **$135,290**[3]  

3. **Computer Systems Analysts**  
   - Typical entry-level education: **Bachelor’s degree**  
   - 2024 median pay: **$111,460**[4]  

The undergraduate field of study most directly associated with preparing workers for many of these roles is **Computer Science** (and closely related majors such as Information Technology or Software Engineering). Typical labor-market and occupational information presented for this field in federal or career resources includes: common occupations for graduates, projected growth and openings, median wages, typical entry-level education, work environment, job duties, and sometimes regional wage/employment data and related fields of study.[1][2]

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, “Computer and Information Technology Occupations,” Occupational Outlook Handbook, 2024–34 projections and pay.  
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, “Software Developers and Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers,” OOH, 2024 data.  
[3] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, “Information Security Analysts,” OOH, 2024 data.  
[4] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, “Computer Systems Analysts,” OOH, 2024 data.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both Agent A and Agent B correctly identify the main occupational group as “Computer and Information Technology Occupations,” so both pass the core entity check.

Next, Width/Completeness: Both agents substantially miss key checklist facts. The correct projected growth rate (15%), annual openings (about 317,700), group median wage ($105,990), and comparison wage ($49,500) are incorrect in both responses. They also fail to use the three specified occupations (Computer and Information Research Scientists; Computer Network Architects; Information Security Analysts with the listed wages) and do not identify the field exactly as “Computer and Information Technology.” These are major factual deviations from the ground truth, not minor rounding errors.

User Experience & Presentation: Both are clearly structured, readable, and reasonably comprehensive, with good formatting and citations. However, since both contain multiple significant statistical inaccuracies on the central data requested, the user would be misled in either case.

Conclusion: Because both agents get the main entity right but fail multiple required factual details (WIDE failures), and neither clearly outperforms the other in accuracy or completeness, this is a LOW-quality tie.

The occupational group is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers** in the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics Occupational Outlook Handbook. This group includes professionals who **design computer applications** (software developers) and those who **identify and report defects in software programs** (quality assurance analysts and testers). It is projected to grow **15% from 2024 to 2034**.[1]

For **software developers**—the application designers—the **2024 median annual pay** was **$133,080**. For **software quality assurance analysts and testers**, the **2024 median annual pay** was **$104,660**.[1]

The **typical entry-level education** for this occupational group is a **bachelor’s degree**. The BLS indicates that **related work experience in a related occupation is typically not required**, and **typical on-the-job training is none**.[1]

The occupation had **1,847,900 jobs in 2024**.[1] Employment is projected to increase by **274,000 jobs** between **2024 and 2034**, and the BLS estimates about **140,100 openings each year**, on average, over the decade.[1]

Common work environments include **computer systems design and related services**, **software publishers**, and **finance and insurance**. These professionals generally work in **office settings or remotely**, often as part of teams with programmers, engineers, project managers, and other IT specialists. Their work typically involves long periods at a computer developing, testing, documenting, and improving software systems and applications.[1][2]

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm

[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics / Occupational Outlook supporting materials for software-related occupations. https://www.bls.gov/oes/
rounds.answer_b:
The occupational group is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers** (U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, SOC 15‑1250), projected to grow **15% from 2024 to 2034**.[1]

Within this group, professionals who design computer applications correspond to **Software Developers (Applications)**. Their **2024 median annual pay** is **$138,800**.[1] Those who identify defects and analyze software quality correspond to **Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers**, with a **2024 median annual pay** of **$101,600**.[1]

For the overall group, the **typical entry-level education** is a **bachelor’s degree**, usually in computer science, software engineering, or a related field.[1][2] The BLS lists **no required related work experience** for entry-level positions and **no formal on-the-job training requirement**, though short-term orientation or employer-specific tools training is common.[1]

In **2024, total employment** for Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers is **2,089,300 jobs**.[1] The **projected employment change, 2024–2034**, is an increase of **306,000 jobs**, reaching about **2,395,300**.[1] The **average number of annual openings** over the decade is projected at **approximately 160,300**, arising from growth and replacement needs.[1]

These professionals typically work in **offices or hybrid/remote settings** in industries such as computer systems design, finance and insurance, software publishing, manufacturing, and enterprise IT departments.[2] Work is generally full time, often with teamwork, deadlines, and occasional overtime during critical release cycles.[2]

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook – Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers, 2024–2034 projections and pay.  
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook – Nature of Work and Work Environment sections for Software Developers and QA roles.

Although both are well-structured and readable, Agent A is slightly more accurate overall. Since Agent A still contains several incorrect statistics, it cannot be rated MUCH_BETTER—only BETTER. Agent B loses primarily due to WIDE detail inaccuracies.

rounds.answer_a:
The combined U.S. occupational category is **Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers**. In the 2024 federal occupational data, the **median annual pay for software developers** was **$133,080**, while the **median annual pay for software quality assurance analysts and testers** was **$104,660**.[1] For the **combined occupation overall**, the highlighted **2024 median annual wage** was **$131,450**.[1]

For entry into this occupation, the **typical education required** is a **bachelor’s degree**.[1] The Bureau of Labor Statistics indicates that **related work experience in a related occupation is typically not required**, and **typical on-the-job training is none**.[1]

In employment terms, this occupational grouping accounted for **1,847,900 jobs in 2024**.[1] The BLS projects the occupation to grow **15 percent from 2024 to 2034**.[1] In numeric terms, that equals an expected **employment increase of 274,000 jobs** over the decade.[1] The projected **average number of annual openings** is **140,100 per year** during the 2024–2034 period.[1]

This grouping combines professionals who create and maintain software applications and systems with those who evaluate software performance, detect defects, and help ensure products meet quality standards before release.[1] It is one of the larger and faster-growing computer occupations in the federal outlook data, reflecting continuing demand for new applications, system upgrades, cybersecurity improvements, and software quality assurance across the economy.[1][2]

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers.” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm

[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics / related occupational data for software occupations. https://www.bls.gov/oes/
rounds.answer_b:
The relevant U.S. occupational grouping is **“Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers”** (SOC 15‑1250) in the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) Occupational Outlook Handbook.[1]

Within this combined occupation, BLS reports **2024 median annual pay** of **$138,800 for software developers** and **$101,600 for software quality assurance analysts and testers**.[1] The BLS highlights an **overall 2024 median pay of $132,800** for the combined occupation.[1]

For this group, the **typical entry-level education** is a **bachelor’s degree**, most often in computer science, software engineering, or a closely related field.[1] The BLS indicates **no required related work experience** for entry-level roles and **no formal on-the-job training requirement**, though new hires often receive short-term orientation to employer-specific tools and processes.[1]

In **2024, total employment** for Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers is reported as **2,089,300 jobs**.[1] Employment in this combined category is projected to **grow 15% from 2024 to 2034**, which is “much faster than the average” for all occupations.[1] Numerically, this reflects an expected **employment change of +306,000 jobs** over the decade, increasing to about **2,395,300 jobs by 2034**.[1]

BLS further projects an **average of about 160,300 job openings per year** from 2024 to 2034, arising from both new job growth and the need to replace workers who leave the occupation (retirements, career changes, etc.).[1]

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers,” 2024–2034 projections and pay. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the combined occupation as “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers,” so both pass the DEEP logic check. However, on WIDTH/completeness and numerical accuracy, both contain errors. Agent A correctly reports the developer median pay ($133,080), the overall median ($131,450), growth rate (15%), and entry requirements, but misstates QA pay, total jobs, projected change, and annual openings. Agent B, by contrast, provides incorrect figures for developer pay, QA pay, overall median pay, total jobs, projected employment change, and annual openings—nearly all key quantitative fields except the growth rate and education/training details. Thus, while both fail WIDE aggregation, Agent B’s inaccuracies are more extensive and farther from the ground truth. In formatting and clarity, both are readable and structured, but Agent A’s figures align more closely with the official data. Therefore, Agent A provides the better overall user experience despite its errors.
